author: @HalcyonSerenade
text:
Can someone remind me how this masterpiece vignette, with _wildly_ positive reception, got its main-game development axed?

[Edit: oh, right... because Hideo Kojima left Konami. That's a pretty good reason, I guess.]

Like, I _hate_ horror and yet I'm _fascinated_ with how well this builds tension just by playing mind games with the player.  There are so many tired horror game tropes used here, like the "door closes by itself" thing, yet they're all played *so damn well* that it's just as horrifying as those tricks are supposed to be!  It's a _master class_ on proper use of visuals, audio, level design, and minimalistic gameplay to build _exquisite_ tension-release cycles.  A full game with the same level of craftsmanship might've been _immensely_ influential to the horror game genre.

author: @IXCipher
text:
I hate how people always talk about jump-scares in a negative manner. It's understandable, as many games/films rely on scaring people through quick surprises, but now whenever people see them, even in very tight moderation -- they instantly complain with, "Oh my God, they're relying on jump scares", completely forgetting everything else the game/film had to offer, just because they want to follow this jump-scare-bashing meta.

I don't agree with over-using jump scares, but they are not inherently bad and can be placed well for effect.﻿
